question_text: A ray of light hits a glass block at an angle of incidence of 30°. The angle of refraction inside the glass is 20°. A student then shines the same light ray at the glass from inside the block, so it hits the glass-air boundary at an angle of incidence of 20°. What will happen to this ray?

options.label: A
options.text: It will refract out of the glass at an angle of refraction of 30°

options.label: B
options.text: It will refract out of the glass at an angle of refraction of 20°

options.label: C
options.text: It will undergo total internal reflection because the angle is too small

options.label: D
options.text: It will refract out of the glass at an angle of refraction of 40°

correct_answer: A) It will refract out of the glass at an angle of refraction of 30°
explanation: Light paths are reversible, meaning if light bends from 30° to 20° going into glass, it will bend from 20° back to 30° going out. The angle of incidence and refraction swap positions when light reverses direction through the same boundary.
